mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 5 Series 540d engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned B57 engine typically costs £5,000–£7,500 supply only for the pre‑LCI generation, with LCI and G60 units slightly higher. Fitting adds £1,200–£1,700. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your 540d.

Yes — the B57 is widely considered a significant improvement over the N57. It's a robust, well‑engineered straight‑six diesel. The main watchpoints are EGR cooler leaks (subject to recall), oil leaks, and DPF/AdBlue issues — all manageable with proper maintenance.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 540d engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: EGR cooler leaks (the most significant issue, subject to recall), oil leaks (valve cover gasket, oil filter housing), turbocharger wear, fuel injector problems, DPF clogging, carbon build‑up, and potential timing chain stretch on high‑mileage units.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a 540d engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value. A G30 540d is a £20,000–£40,000 vehicle — a £6,000 recon is 15–30% of the car's value, making replacement a clear decision. This is well within our 70% BMW scrap threshold.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW 540d engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The 540d engine code is B57D30 (pre‑LCI, 2017–2020), B57D30B (LCI, 2020–2023), or B57 (G60, 2023+). Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I prevent the common failures on a 540d?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — check for EGR cooler recall completion (especially on 2016–2018 models). Regular long runs for DPF health, BMW‑spec oil changes with shortened intervals (5,000–7,500 miles), and monitoring coolant levels are the most effective prevention. If you notice coolant loss, white smoke, or oil spots, book an inspection immediately.

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the range across all B57 generations; specific costs vary by era — see the Era Map above.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a 540d engine typically takes 12–16 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). Engine oil capacity is 6.5 litres, and coolant capacity is 11.7–11.9 litres.

problems.title: EGR Cooler Leak (All Generations — Subject to Recall)
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss, white exhaust smoke, rough idling, burning smell from engine bay, engine warning light.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught before overheating)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you notice coolant loss or burning smell, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: The B57's EGR cooler is the most significant weakness — BMW issued a recall for this affecting 2016–2018 models. If you notice coolant loss, white smoke, or a burning smell, have the EGR cooler inspected immediately. A cracked EGR cooler can leak coolant into the intake system, causing serious engine damage. In extreme cases, it can create a fire risk. Many owners choose to have the EGR cooler replaced with updated parts under recall.

problems.title: Oil Leaks (Valve Cover Gasket / Oil Filter Housing)
problems.symptoms: Oil spots under the vehicle, burning oil smell, drop in oil level, visible oil residue on the engine.
problems.recommendation: Oil leaks are one of the most frequently reported B57 issues. The valve cover gasket and oil filter housing are the most common sources. If you notice oil spots or a burning smell, have the gaskets inspected. Replacing them promptly prevents low oil levels and potential engine damage.

problems.title: DPF Clogging / AdBlue System Issues (Short Journey Use)
problems.symptoms: DPF warning light, reduced engine performance, increased fuel consumption, AdBlue warning light.
problems.repairCost: £300–£800 (forced regeneration or DPF clean); £500–£1,500 (AdBlue system repair)
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (unless DPF is physically damaged)
problems.recommendation: Like all modern diesels, the B57 needs regular long runs for passive DPF regeneration. If your 540d is used primarily for short journeys, plan a 30–45 minute motorway run every two weeks. The AdBlue system can also develop faults — if you see AdBlue warnings, have the system checked promptly.

rows.whenItMakesSense: Catch it early — before coolant contamination causes damage
Regular long runs — forced regen or clean
Often software — can be resolved; hardware faults require specialist
Less common than N57; viable on lower‑mileage examples
Little left to salvage — replacement is the only option

buyingChecks.title: Buying This 540d? Check These First
buyIf: * EGR cooler recall work completed (especially on 2016–2018 models)
* No coolant loss or white smoke — EGR cooler in good condition
* No oil leaks — valve cover and filter housing dry
* Documented DPF regeneration history or regular long‑distance driving
* Full BMW service history with correct oil spec
avoidIf: * Coolant loss with no diagnosis — EGR cooler may be failing — walk away
* White smoke from exhaust — potential EGR cooler failure
* Oil spots under the vehicle — oil leaks may be significant
* Engine warning light with no diagnosis — potentially expensive

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The B57 is BMW's finest modern straight‑six diesel — a 3.0‑litre twin‑turbo with 320–340 hp, 680–700 Nm and genuine performance credentials. It's significantly more reliable than the N57 it replaced. The main watchpoints — EGR cooler leaks (subject to recall), oil leaks, DPF and AdBlue issues — are all manageable with proper maintenance. Buy on full service history and check for EGR cooler recall completion. A well‑maintained 540d is a long‑lived, torque‑rich executive express.

cta.label: → Compare 540d engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW 5 Series 540d (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Coolant Level — Check for unexplained loss; EGR cooler leak is the most common B57 issue
* White Smoke — Check on startup; EGR cooler failure indicator
* Oil Leaks — Check under the car and around the engine; valve cover and filter housing leaks are common
* Cold Start — Listen for rattling; timing chain stretch less common but possible on high‑mileage units
* Service History — including which generation (pre‑LCI, LCI, or G60) and confirmed maintenance
* DPF Status — Check for warning lights; short journeys can cause clogging
* AdBlue System — Check for warning lights; AdBlue system faults are common
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved issues?
